critique.summary: The article introduces an interactive 3D simulator of the three-body problem that runs in a web browser. It provides presets and adjustable parameters for simulating gravitational interactions between three bodies in space.
mainPoints: The simulator models the three-body problem in three dimensions.
It runs directly in the browser without requiring additional software.
Users can adjust parameters such as gravitational constant, softness, and time step.
The tool includes presets to help users explore different scenarios.
critique.truthfulness: The claims about the existence of a browser-based interactive 3D three-body problem simulator are accurate and verifiable by visiting the provided URL. The description of adjustable parameters and presets aligns with typical features of physics simulators. There are no extraordinary or unsupported claims made in the article.
considerations: The article does not discuss the numerical methods or integrators used in the simulation, which are important for accuracy and stability.
There is no mention of the limitations or potential inaccuracies inherent in simulating chaotic systems like the three-body problem.
The article does not provide any external references or sources to validate the scientific correctness of the simulation.

comments_critique.summary: The discussion centers on a browser-based 3D three-body problem simulator, with users commenting on its UI, simulation accuracy, and solver methods. Some users share similar projects and suggest improvements, while others express skepticism about the originality of the post.

comments.author: hazrmard
comments.text: Very cool! Interesting how the choice of solver affects the solution. Euler doesn't handle misbehaved equations very well. You can see this in the Helix setup where the bodies just fly off.
comments.analysis: This comment accurately highlights the impact of numerical solvers on simulation stability, specifically noting Euler's limitations, which is a well-known fact in numerical analysis.

comments.author: efilife
comments.text: OP's account posts LLM generated comments almost exclusively, this is likely not an exception
comments.analysis: The claim about the OP's account posting mostly LLM-generated comments may be true but is unverifiable from the discussion alone; it introduces skepticism about the post's authenticity.

comments.author: koolala
comments.text: When I hit Random 3d to see a normal three-body they yeet out of existence far far away.
comments.analysis: This observation about the simulation's behavior suggests potential issues with initial conditions or solver stability, consistent with known chaotic behavior in three-body problems.

comments.author: ubezon
comments.text: Very cool! nice to be able to export json too
comments.analysis: This positive feedback highlights a useful feature (JSON export), contributing constructively to the discussion.

comments.author: vivzkestrel
comments.text:
- i have seen a tonne of these

- would be nice if someone at this point actually made a tutorial on how to go about making one from scratch with threejs
comments.analysis: The suggestion for a tutorial is constructive and indicates a desire for educational content, though it does not evaluate the current project directly.

comments.author: neondude
comments.text: Feedback : The ui covers more than half my screen on mobile browser
comments.analysis: This comment provides useful user experience feedback about UI design on mobile devices.

comments.author: hermitcrab
comments.text: Random3D seems to always have all the stars heading off to infinity.
comments.analysis: This observation aligns with known chaotic dynamics in three-body simulations and may indicate issues with the random initial conditions or solver.

comments.author: modeless
comments.text: How many of these are there? https://trisolarchaos.com/
comments.analysis: The comment seeks to contextualize the project by referencing similar existing work, adding value to the discussion.

comments.author: curseofcasandra
comments.text: The tri-Solarians could have just used this!
comments.analysis: This humorous comment references science fiction but adds little substantive value to the technical discussion.

comments.author: kordlessagain
comments.analysis: This brief lament lacks context or explanation, making it unhelpful for understanding or improving the project.

comments.author: kurthong
comments.text: Had a similar idea and built a prototype for it. :)
comments.analysis: Sharing personal experience is constructive but the comment is vague and lacks detail.

comments.author: kurthong
comments.text: https://solgrbit.d9ng.co.kr/ Had a similar idea and built a prototype for it.
comments.analysis: Providing a link to a similar project adds value, though the comment could be more detailed.

comments.author: westurner
comments.text: Notes re: Multi-body problems on "Show HN: Browser-based interactive 3D Three-Body problem simulator" (2025) https://news.ycombinator.com/item?id=45981336 which has Velocity Verlet integration method, 4th-order Runge-Kutta (RK4), and Kepler solvers
comments.analysis: This comment provides relevant technical information about solvers used in similar projects, enriching the discussion.
